one_line_definition: 8-pin IC implementing three timer modes (astable, monostable, bistable) via internal comparators and flip-flop
555 operating mode where output oscillates continuously; frequency set by R1, R2, C
555 mode producing a single output pulse of duration 1.1·R·C per trigger event
555 mode where output latches high or low based on set/reset inputs; used for debouncing
Distributed electrical structure for guiding electromagnetic waves when line length is a significant fraction of wavelength
Coupled PDEs ∂V/∂z = −(R+jωL)I and ∂I/∂z = −(G+jωC)V describing voltage and current on a distributed line
Ratio of forward voltage to forward current wave on a transmission line; Z0 = √(L/C) for lossless lines
Ratio of reflected to incident wave amplitude at a discontinuity; Γ = (ZL−Z0)/(ZL+Z0)
Time-space diagram tracking forward and reflected wave amplitudes at each reflection event
Complex wavenumber γ = α + jβ = √[(R+jωL)(G+jωC)]; α = attenuation, β = phase constant
Ratio of voltage maximum to minimum on a standing wave; VSWR = (1+|Γ|)/(1−|Γ|)
Impedance seen looking into a transmission line of length l terminated in ZL; Zin = Z0·(ZL+jZ0tanβl)/(Z0+jZLtanβl)
Transmission line section of length λ/4 that transforms ZL to Z0²/ZL; acts as impedance inverter
Network description based on traveling wave amplitudes; S_ij = b_i/a_j with all other ports matched
Graphical representation of signal paths in a network used with Mason's Rule to compute gain
Formula for computing transfer function of a signal flow graph as ratio of forward path and loop gain products
Ratio of power delivered to load to available source power; GT = |S21|²(1−|ΓS|²)(1−|ΓL|²)/|denominator|²
Graphical tool mapping complex impedance to reflection coefficient plane; enables transmission line and matching calculations
Circle on Smith Chart representing all impedances with a given normalized resistance r; center (r/(r+1), 0)
Smith Chart rotated 180° to work in admittance; used for adding shunt elements
Impedance matching technique using a shunt transmission line stub to cancel susceptance at a chosen point on SWR circle
Ratio of energy stored to energy dissipated per radian; Q = ω0·(energy stored)/power_dissipated
Resonant circuit with R, L, C in series; ω0 = 1/√(LC); 3dB bandwidth Δω = ω0/Q
Resonant circuit with R, L, C in parallel; same ω0; Q = Rp/(ω0L)
Piezoelectric quartz element with Q ~ 10⁵–10⁶ and ppm-level frequency stability; modeled as series LCR branch with parallel Cp
Micromachined mechanical resonator with Q > 10⁴; potential for on-chip high-Q frequency references
Two-element reactive network transforming impedance by ratio n; Q = √(n−1)
Power lost in a matching network due to finite component Q; IL = 1/(1 + Q_L/Q_c)
Three-element matching network (shunt-series-shunt) with extra degree of freedom for Q control
Three-element matching network (series-shunt-series) with extra degree of freedom for Q control
Two-port description I = Y·V; suited to shunt-shunt feedback topology analysis
Two-port description V = Z·I; suited to series-series feedback topology analysis
Two-port description cascaded by matrix multiplication; useful for multi-section networks and T-lines
K = (1−|S11|²−|S22|²+|Δ|²)/(2|S12S21|); K > 1 and |Δ| < 1 → unconditionally stable two-port
Circles on Smith Chart bounding source/load impedance regions that cause oscillation in a potentially unstable device
Ratio of power delivered to load to power input to two-port; Gp = P_L/P_in; independent of source impedance
Ratio of available output power to available source power; Ga = P_avn/P_avs; independent of load impedance
Simultaneous conjugate matching of input and output ports to maximize transducer gain GT
Maximum GT achievable when K > 1; MAG = |S21/S12|·(K−√(K²−1))
|S21/S12|; gain limit when K = 1 (boundary of conditional stability)
F = SNR_in/SNR_out; NF = 10·log(F) dB; measures degradation of SNR through a device
F_total = F1 + (F2−1)/G1 + (F3−1)/(G1·G2) + ...; first-stage NF dominates cascade noise figure
MDS = −174 dBm + NF + 10log(B) + SNR_min; minimum signal power detectable above noise
Noise in resistors due to thermal agitation of carriers; vn² = 4kTRΔf; white noise
Noise due to discrete carrier crossings of a potential barrier; id² = 2qIDC·Δf; proportional to DC current
Low-frequency noise with PSD ∝ 1/f; dominant below corner frequency; originates from trap states
id² = 4kTγgds0·Δf in saturation; γ = 2/3 for long-channel; increases for short-channel devices
Equivalent resistance characterizing magnitude of input-referred voltage noise of a two-port; Rn ∝ γ/gm for FETs
Lowest achievable noise figure of a two-port; Fmin = 1 + 2√(Rn·Gu); achieved at Yopt
Source admittance that minimizes noise figure to Fmin; Yopt = Gopt + jBopt
Circles on Smith Chart of constant noise figure; centered near Yopt; used for noise-gain trade-off
Adding series inductor Ls at FET source; adds real input impedance ωT·Ls without resistive noise → enables noise-lossless matching
Common-source + common-gate stack; reduces Miller effect, improves reverse isolation S12, near-zero cascode noise contribution
Frequency at which current gain = 1; fT = gm/(2π·Cgs); key figure of merit for RF transistors
Output approximated as so = a1·si + a2·si² + a3·si³; coefficients from Taylor expansion of device I-V
Distortion at harmonic frequencies; HD2 = a2·A/(2a1), HD3 = a3·A²/(4a1) for single-tone input
Distortion products at beat frequencies from two-tone input; IM3 at 2ω1−ω2 grows as A³
Input power where gain drops 1 dB below linear extrapolation; P_in,−1dB ≈ IIP3 − 9.6 dB
Extrapolated input power where fundamental and IM3 product powers are equal; IIP3 = √(4a1/(3|a3|))
Extrapolated input power where fundamental and IM2 product powers are equal; critical for direct-conversion receivers
1/IIP3_total ≈ 1/IIP3_1 + G1/IIP3_2 + ...; later high-gain stages typically dominate
Large out-of-band signal compresses LNA gain, reducing sensitivity for the wanted in-band signal
Negative feedback reduces b2 by (1+T)³ and reduces b3; but second-order interaction 2a2²f term can cause distortion null
Series resistor (or inductor) at emitter/source; increases linearity and input impedance; reduces gain
Shifting signal spectrum from RF to IF (or vice versa) via multiplication by LO signal
Frequency that maps to the same IF as the desired signal; must be filtered or cancelled
Receiver architecture with one or more IF stages; image-reject filter before first mixer; dominant classical receiver topology
Receiver architecture with LO = RF frequency; down-converts directly to DC; challenges: DC offset, 1/f noise, LO leakage
I/Q mixer using 90° phase shift and summation to cancel image signal; IRR limited by I/Q mismatch
Ratio of desired signal power to image signal power after mixing; IRR ≈ −10log[(δA/A)²+(δθ)²]/4
Cross-coupled switching quad driven by LO commutating the RF gm stage current; conversion gain = (4/π)·gm·RL
Ratio of IF output amplitude to RF input amplitude for a mixer; gc = (2/π)·gm·RL (single-balanced)
Mixer using MOS switches without DC bias; low 1/f noise, high IIP3, ~4 dB conversion loss
Switched-capacitor filter using N clock phases; achieves high-Q bandpass response centered at LO frequency
Cadence SpectreRF analyses for periodic circuits: periodic steady-state, periodic AC (conversion gain), periodic noise (mixer NF)
Duplex mode where TX and RX operate simultaneously on different frequencies; requires duplexer
Duplex mode where TX and RX alternate in time slots; simpler RF hardware than FDD

aliases_seen: peak-to-average power ratio, PAR, crest factor
one_line_definition: Ratio of peak to average power in a signal; high PAPR (e.g., OFDM) forces PA back-off, reducing efficiency
Measure of I/Q modulator accuracy; EVM = |error vector|/|ideal vector|; spec typically < −30 dB
Power leaked into adjacent frequency channel due to PA nonlinearity; must meet transmit spectral mask
PAE = (Pout−Pin)/PDC; overall efficiency metric for power amplifiers accounting for input power consumption
Load resistance that maximizes output power for given supply voltage; Ropt = VCC²/(2·Pout)
Parasitic series inductance (~1 nH/mm) from bond wires; limits voltage swing and efficiency in RF PAs
PA packaging using solder bumps instead of bond wires; LE < 0.1 nH; required for >10 GHz designs
Linear PA with 360° conduction angle; max efficiency 50% with inductive load
PA with 180° conduction angle using half-sinusoid current pulses; max efficiency π/4 ≈ 78.5%
PA with conduction angle between 180° and 360°; practical compromise between linearity and efficiency
PA with conduction angle < 180°; efficiency → 100% as conduction angle → 0°; suitable only for constant-envelope signals
Fraction of a cycle (in degrees) during which the PA transistor conducts; determines PA class
Normalized output power Pout/(Vmax·Imax); 0.125 for both Class A and Class B; peaks at Class AB
Technique adjusting PA supply voltage to follow signal envelope; maintains near-peak efficiency across PAR
PA technique separating AM (supply-modulated Class C) and PM (phase-modulated RF) paths
Loop gain magnitude = 1 and phase = 0° (mod 360°) at oscillation frequency; necessary for sustained oscillation
Product of forward gain and feedback factor in an oscillator; determines stability and oscillation amplitude
Oscillator using inductor-capacitor resonator as frequency-selective feedback element; Q determines phase noise
Oscillator using capacitive voltage divider for feedback; feedback ratio 1/n = C1/(C1+C2); avoids squegging
CE or CC version of Colpitts oscillator; standard topology for crystal oscillators in CMOS
Random fluctuation in oscillator phase; appears as noise skirts around carrier; L(Δω) in dBc/Hz
Oscillator using RC charge/discharge between voltage thresholds; simpler but high phase noise
Odd number of inverter stages forming oscillator; period = 2·N·tpd; used in PLLs and clock generators
Parasitic low-frequency oscillation in PA/oscillator bias circuitry; prevented by CE ≤ n·CT
Oscillator analyzed by requiring active circuit negative conductance to cancel tank loss conductance G' = G
CMOS differential pair with drain-gate cross connections generating negative conductance Gx = −gm/2
Oscillator whose frequency is tuned by a control voltage via a varactor; key element in PLLs
Reverse-biased PN junction used as voltage-variable capacitor; Cj = Cj0/(1−V/ψ0)^n
Gate capacitor varying between Cmin (depletion) and Cox (accumulation) with gate voltage; accumulation-mode preferred for high Q
Array of capacitors switched by MOSFET switches for coarse VCO frequency tuning; optimal W/L balances Ron and Coff
Feedback system locking VCO phase/frequency to a reference; contains VCO, divider, phase detector, loop filter
PLL-based circuit generating precise, tunable output frequency as integer multiple of crystal reference; fout = N·fref
